plan.contextChunk:
property: conditional on $x_2$, variable $x_3$ is independent of
variable $x_1$. The joint probability of all the variables shown here is
$p(x_1, x_2, x_3, y_1, y_2, y_3) =  \phi_{12}(x_1, x_2)  \phi_{23}(x_2, x_3)
\psi_{1}(y_1, x_1) \psi_{2}(y_2, x_2) \psi_{3}(y_3, x_3)$. Using
$p(a,b) = p(a \bigm | b) p(b)$, we can also write the probability of the
$x$ variables conditioned on the observations $y$,
$$p(x_1, x_2, x_3 \bigm | y_1, y_2, y_3) =  
\frac{1}{p(\mathbf{y})} \phi_{12}(x_1, x_2)  \phi_{23}(x_2, x_3) \psi_{1}(y_1, x_1) \psi_{2}(y_2, x_2) \psi_{3}(y_3, x_3).$$
For brevity, we write $p(\mathbf{y})$ for $p(y_1, y_2, y_3)$. Thus, to
form the conditional distribution, within a normalization factor, we
simply include the observed variable values into the joint probability.
For vision applications, we often use such Markov chain structures to
describe events over time.

To capture relationships over space, a two-dimensional (2D) structure is
useful, called a **Markov random field** (MRF) @Blake2011:

![Two-dimensional Markov random field.](figures/graphical_models/mrf.png){width="48%" #fig-leafs}

Then the joint probability over all the variables factorizes into this
product: 

$$p(\mathbf{x} \bigm | \mathbf{y}) = 
\frac{1}{p(\mathbf{y})}
\prod_{(i,j)} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \prod_i \psi_i(x_i, y_i),$$
where the first product is over all spatial neighbors $i$ and $j$, and the second
product is over all nodes $i$.

An example of how Markov random field models are applied to images is
depicted in @fig-leafs, which show a small image region and its context
in the image (@fig-leafs [a]). @fig-leafs [b] shows an MRF with each
node corresponding to a pixel of the image region. The states of an MRF
can be indicator variables of image segment membership for each pixel.
The states of a hypothetical most-probable configuration of this MRF are
shown as the color of each node in this illustration.

:::{#fig-leafs layout-ncol="2"}
![](figures/graphical_models/leaf2.jpg){width=97% #fig-leafs-a}

![](figures/graphical_models/leaf1.jpg){width=100% #fig-leafs-b}

Fig (a) Image to be segmented and a local region of (a) marked in red.  (b) Visualization of an MRF of nodes corresponding to image pixels inside the marked region, with states indicating segment membership, for a hypothetical most probable configuration.
::: 

## Directed Graphical Models

In addition to undirected graphical models, another type of graphical
model is commonly used. **Directed graphical models** @Koller2009
describe factorizations of the joint probability into products of
conditional probability distributions. Each node in a directed graph
contributes a well-specified factor in the joint probability: the
probability of its variable, conditioned on all the variables originating
arrows pointing into it. So this graph

![A directed graphical model with three variables.](figures/graphical_models/directed.png){width="20%" #eq-directedjoint}

denotes the joint probability,

elements: A 3x3 horizontal grid of latent nodes (white spheres) in the XZ plane representing hidden variables X_ij
A 3x3 horizontal grid of observed nodes (grey spheres) elevated vertically along the Y-axis directly above each latent node, representing observations Y_ij
Horizontal grid lines (cylinder edges) connecting adjacent latent nodes along the X and Z axes
Vertical edges (cylinder lines) connecting each latent node X_ij to its corresponding observed node Y_ij
Active belief state colors (glow halos or color fills) on the latent nodes indicating their current segmentation category (e.g., foreground-green, background-blue)
Pulsing probability wavefronts or particle streams flowing along edges when demonstrating belief propagation
Text labels: 'y1', 'y2', 'y3' next to the back-row observed nodes, and 'x1', 'x2', 'x3' next to the back-row latent nodes
A highlight outline (semi-transparent sphere) representing a selected/conditioned node

interactions.label: Spatial Coupling (φ)
interactions.teaches: Adjusts the strength of spatial compatibility between neighboring latent nodes; higher coupling forces neighbors to agree, smoothing the resulting segmentation.

interactions.label: Conditioning
interactions.teaches: Switches the conditioning state to visualize how evidence propagates or how conditioning on a node isolates its neighbors.

demo_steps.title: The 2D MRF Grid
demo_steps.narration: In a 2D Markov Random Field, latent variables X (white) represent hidden states like true pixel segmentations. Each latent node is connected to its spatial neighbors and to a noisy local observation Y (grey) directly above it.

demo_steps.title: Observing the Evidence
demo_steps.narration: By switching to 'Observe Y', the observed nodes (grey) are clamped to noisy measurements. Their values act as local unary potentials (ψ), pulling the states of the hidden X nodes toward matching colors.

demo_steps.title: Smoothing via Spatial Coupling
demo_steps.narration: Increase the spatial coupling (φ) to force neighboring latent nodes to align. Notice how local noise is smoothed away, creating contiguous segment patches — a critical feature for computer vision segmentation.

demo_steps.title: Conditional Independence
demo_steps.narration: Fix the center latent node. Notice how the flow of influence is blocked: when this node is observed, its direct neighbors become conditionally independent of each other, preventing beliefs from propagating through the center.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: An elevated 3D orthographic view looking from the front-left toward the back-right, revealing the 2D grid structure below and the vertical connections to the observed nodes above.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) The dominant baseline is the grid of X nodes. In the source image, the row-lines run left-to-right (skewed up slightly) and the column-lines recede diagonally up and to the right. (2) This receding pattern indicates the camera is positioned at a diagonal azimuth looking from the bottom-left of the grid toward the top-right, implying an azimuth around -35 degrees relative to the primary axes. (3) The vertical offset lines from X to Y are drawn straight up and down, but we look 'down' onto the grid surface, which implies an elevation of roughly 25-30 degrees.

camera_view.view_notes: Maintains the characteristic perspective of the original textbook diagram where vertical lines go straight up, columns recede at an angle, and both layers of nodes are clearly visible.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must closely replicate the original diagram's node-link silhouette and spatial layout
inline_constraints.visible_ui: Position controls unobtrusively at the bottom edge of the canvas with no background plate
inline_constraints.explainers: Use interactive hover tooltips on individual nodes to display their local potential formulas, e.g., psi_i(x_i, y_i)
inline_constraints.framing: Ensure ample vertical clearance for the upper Y nodes so labels do not clip
inline_constraints.camera: Match the isometric-like perspective of the source illustration

interactionPlan.notes: Use a subtle glowing shader for latent nodes to represent state probability distributions. When 'Observe Y' is active, render particles or dashed flows traveling from Y down to X, then spreading outward through horizontal grid lines. If 'Fix Center X' is selected, color the center node with a solid boundary ring to indicate its fixed state, and dim the connection lines passing through it to show blocked propagation paths.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3x3 MRF grid from an elevated, front-left oblique perspective. The rows of the grid run parallel to the page horizontal and recede obliquely into the background, making the left-to-right ordering of the columns (1, 2, 3) clearly sequential from left to right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the same 3x3 grid rotated by approximately 45 degrees, forming a diamond-like projection where the front-most corner of the grid points directly at the camera. The camera is highly elevated looking down-left.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The camera azimuth in the rendered screenshot is rotated relative to the source, causing the columns to orient along diagonals rather than parallel to the page. This scrambles the visual left-to-right sequence of the labeled nodes, making column 3 appear on the far left and column 1 near the top-center.
discrepancies: The 3x3 grid is rotated 45 degrees (diamond layout) compared to the source's row-aligned oblique layout.
Because of the grid rotation, the labels x1, x2, x3 and y1, y2, y3 are out of order from left to right (y3 is on the far left, while in the source y1 is on the far left).
There is an interactive glowing ring and particle system on the center node which is not present in the static source figure.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive MRF visualization with high fidelity, but the camera rotation scrambles the natural left-to-right indexing of the labeled nodes.
action_items: Rotate the grid or adjust the camera's azimuthDeg in setCameraView to match the source figure's row-aligned oblique perspective.
Ensure that x1/y1 are positioned on the far left, x2/y2 in the middle, and x3/y3 on the far right to restore the logical ordering shown in the textbook.
Adjust the label offsets so that x1, x2, x3 are clearly positioned directly above their respective white nodes without overlapping other edges.

feedback.rationale: The concept and interactivity are highly rated, but the camera rotation and label alignments are slightly off, which can be easily resolved by refining the generator's camera setup and label positioning.
action_items: Rotate the grid or adjust the camera's azimuthDeg in setCameraView to match the source figure's row-aligned oblique perspective.
Ensure that x1/y1 are positioned on the far left, x2/y2 in the middle, and x3/y3 on the far right to restore the logical ordering shown in the textbook.
Adjust the label offsets so that x1, x2, x3 are clearly positioned directly above their respective white nodes without overlapping other edges.
actionItems: Rotate the grid or adjust the camera's azimuthDeg in setCameraView to match the source figure's row-aligned oblique perspective.
Ensure that x1/y1 are positioned on the far left, x2/y2 in the middle, and x3/y3 on the far right to restore the logical ordering shown in the textbook.
Adjust the label offsets so that x1, x2, x3 are clearly positioned directly above their respective white nodes without overlapping other edges.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3x3 grid of Markov Random Field nodes using an oblique projection, where horizontal lines are perfectly horizontal, vertical lines go straight up, and depth lines recede up and right. The viewpoint is from the front-left, looking slightly down at the grid.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot matches the source's oblique projection precisely, utilizing an orthographic camera view where horizontal grid lines remain horizontal and depth lines recede at a matching angle.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are practically identical, successfully capturing the exact 3D grid perspective and alignment of the textbook illustration.

shape_comparison.rendered_shape: 3D sphere with black outline shader effect

shape_comparison.source_shape: flat grey circle with black outline
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: 3D grey sphere with black outline shader effect

discrepancies: The x1, x2, and x3 labels overlap slightly with the diagonal grid lines and nodes, whereas they are cleanly separated in the source.
The grid lines (cylinders) are relatively thick compared to the thin lines in the source.
The center node has an interactive yellow highlight/glow that is not present in the static source image.
evaluation.notes: Excellent interactive reproduction of the MRF diagram with extremely faithful orthographic camera alignment, though label placement could be slightly adjusted.
action_items: Adjust the offset of x1, x2, and x3 labels to prevent them from overlapping the diagonal grid lines.
Slightly reduce the radius of the cylinder geometries used for the grid edges to match the thin line aesthetic of the original diagram.
Disable or dim the yellow center node highlight on initial load (Joint state) so it starts in a state that matches the clean original textbook figure perfectly.

feedback.rationale: The figure is highly accurate with an overall score of 4.6, and the minor visual polish issues like label offsets and cylinder thickness do not justify further iteration.

gpt-5.5.failure_modes_analysis: The generated figure reproduces the basic 3x3 latent/observed lattice with white and gray circular nodes, black edges, and labels y1–y3 / x1–x3. However, it adds visible UI controls, a reset button, a translucent yellow highlight/ring around the center node, and code for colored halos/particles/conditioning states that are not present in the source. The latent-grid depth spacing and observation-edge proportions appear compressed/exaggerated compared with the source, and the generated x labels are lowercase rather than the uppercase X labels shown in the original.
gpt-5.5.failure_modes_reasoning: Hallucination applies because the rendered output includes non-source elements such as the control panel and the yellow center-node conditioning highlight, with additional colored/animated explanatory elements in the code. Scale-Wrong applies because the relative proportions of the lattice are noticeably altered, especially the observation links and compressed latent-row spacing compared with the original diagram.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_analysis: The scene constructs a real 3D layered MRF graph: a 3×3 lower lattice of latent spherical nodes, a matching elevated 3×3 layer of observed spherical nodes, vertical observation links, horizontal/receding spatial links, boundary stubs, and labels for x1–x3 and y1–y3. The main topology and relative placement are plausible, with nodes and edges connected correctly in 3D. Minor deviations include the added halos, particles, and conditioned highlight that are not core textbook geometry, and the lattice uses an explicit skewed basis that may be more stylized than a clean square grid viewed by the camera, but it is not merely a flat texture or billboard.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major graph elements are present and connected with plausible 3D geometry, with only minor stylization/alignment issues rather than missing or wrong topology.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls, which function for camera drag/zoom but do not count as meaningful interaction. Meaningful controls include a φ slider that updates the displayed value and visibly changes halo strength, edge opacity/thickness, particle scale/speed; a conditioning-state dropdown that visibly switches between Joint, Observe Y, and Fix Center X with node color/halo/highlight and particle changes; hover handling on registered nodes/edges/highlight that shows an internal tooltip; and click handling on registered nodes/edges/highlight that triggers explanatory popups via parent postMessage and often changes the scene through goToStep. There is also a Reset View button that visibly resets the camera, plus continuous particle animation when non-Joint states are selected. A guided demo is implemented as demoSteps/goToStep, but it is not exposed with visible next/step buttons, so it is only indirectly accessible by clicking scene objects or externally calling the function. The UI is compact in the lower-left corner and only minimally overlaps a small boundary stub, not important geometry.
